reviews.review_description:
If you own a VR headset and aren’t playing Monkey Tag, you’re seriously missing out. This is easily one of the best games ever made.

The movement system alone is revolutionary — instead of using buttons, you literally move by swinging your arms, climbing, and jumping like a real monkey. It sounds simple, but it feels insanely immersive and becomes super addictive once you get the hang of it.

What makes this game truly stand out is how fun it is with other people. Whether you’re playing tag, infection, or just messing around with friends, every match turns into chaotic, hilarious moments you can’t get anywhere else. It’s basically a giant social playground where anything can happen.

The community is HUGE too — millions of players have joined, and there’s always someone online to play with. And the best part? It’s easy to learn but actually takes skill to master, so you keep coming back to improve.

On top of that, it’s not just a game — it’s a workout, a hangout spot, and a competitive experience all in one. No other VR game combines all of that this well.

Overall, Monkey Tag isn’t just a game — it’s an experience. Once you start playing, it’s almost impossible to stop.

The game started out like any other—bright colors, fast action, and a steady stream of players eager to win. But beneath the surface, something darker lingered. Every match felt less like fun and more like a battlefield of egos. Players didn’t just compete; they tore each other down. A simple mistake could trigger a flood of insults, and winning only seemed to give people more reason to brag and belittle others.
New players quickly learned that skill wasn’t the only thing you needed to survive—you needed thick skin. The chat was filled with negativity, where encouragement was rare and kindness almost nonexistent. Even friends turned on each other when rankings were at stake. It wasn’t just a game anymore; it was a place where frustration spread faster than teamwork.
Over time, the toxicity changed the experience. What once felt exciting became exhausting. Players logged in not for enjoyment, but out of habit, chasing wins that never felt satisfying. The game itself hadn’t changed—but the community had, and that made all the difference.

reviews.review_title: Good game in general horrible moderation
reviews.review_description: I rate gorilla tag copies and fangames and I’ll truthfully rate this game 2.5 stars. The game itself actually has some good things—there are a lot of game settings, the movement feels good, and features like the speed boost make it fun. However, this is the MOST TOXIC Gorilla Tag fangame/copy I HAVE EVER played. I’ve heard people saying racial slurs and other horrible things, and there seems to be absolutely zero moderation. I even checked the Discord reports where people posted video proof, and there were no responses at all. It’s disappointing because the actual game has potential and can be fun, but the complete lack of moderation makes the experience awful.
